critic_raw:
The task is framed as 'regression / continuous outcome prediction' (quality of life score improvement), yet the baseline model is logistic regression. Logistic regression is a classifier that outputs class probabilities, not a continuous value predictor. Using it as a baseline for a regression target means the two models are solving fundamentally different problems — the comparison is between a continuous predictor and a probability output. This makes it impossible to compare on shared evaluation metrics like MAE or RMSE without an ad hoc conversion step that is not described.

With approximately 1,500 patients over three years, and an RNN with attention mechanisms, the sample-to-parameter ratio is highly unfavorable. An attention-augmented RNN with sequence inputs of variable length across multiple symptom logs and treatment events will have orders of magnitude more parameters than the dataset can support. Overfitting risk is severe, and no regularization strategy is mentioned beyond train/validation/test splitting.

The design uses a stratified random split while acknowledging 'temporal dependencies' exist in patient treatment timelines. If a patient's symptom trajectory at month 6 influences their outcome at month 12, randomly shuffling patients across splits may distribute early observations in training and outcome measurements in test for the same patient — or more critically, the model never encounters out-of-distribution temporal evolution patterns that arise over time (e.g., treatment practice changes, new drugs entering protocol). Concept drift over the three-year span is not addressed.

MAE is chosen as the primary metric for its 'interpretability,' but the design does not define the scale or clinical significance of MAE units on a quality-of-life score. Without a minimally clinically important difference (MCID) benchmark, there is no way to interpret whether the MAE difference between RNN and logistic baseline represents a meaningful clinical improvement or is within noise.
all_issues_raised: The baseline model is logistic regression — a probability classifier — applied to a continuous regression target (quality-of-life score improvement), making direct metric comparison on MAE/RMSE ill-defined without an unreported conversion step.

An attention-augmented RNN trained on approximately 1,500 patient records has an extremely unfavorable sample-to-parameter ratio, with no described regularization strategy beyond data splitting, making overfitting nearly certain.

The stratified random split on longitudinal treatment timeline data ignores concept drift across the three-year span (e.g., treatment protocol changes), so the model is never evaluated on its ability to generalize to temporally out-of-distribution patient trajectories.

MAE is reported without defining clinical significance thresholds (MCID) for the quality-of-life score scale, making it impossible to determine whether the observed metric difference is clinically meaningful or within noise.

An attention-augmented RNN trained on approximately 1,500 patient records has an extremely unfavorable sample-to-parameter ratio, with no described regularization strategy beyond data splitting, making overfitting nearly certain.

The stratified random split on longitudinal treatment timeline data ignores concept drift across the three-year span (e.g., treatment protocol changes), so the model is never evaluated on its ability to generalize to temporally out-of-distribution patient trajectories.

MAE is reported without defining clinical significance thresholds (MCID) for the quality-of-life score scale, making it impossible to determine whether the observed metric difference is clinically meaningful or within noise.
